content: Which of the following statements about threads is/are TRUE?

options.content: Threads can only be implemented in kernel space
Each thread has its own file descriptor table for open files
All the threads belonging to a process share a common stack
Threads belonging to a process are by default not protected from each other

explanation:
Let's analyze each of the statements one by one:

Option A: Threads can only be implemented in kernel space

This statement is not true. Threads can be implemented in different ways, including user space and kernel space. User space threads are managed without kernel intervention, whereas kernel threads are managed by the operating system kernel.

Option B: Each thread has its own file descriptor table for open files

This statement is also not true. In most operating systems, threads within the same process share the same file descriptor table. This allows threads to access files opened by other threads within the same process.

Option C: All the threads belonging to a process share a common stack

This statement is false. Although threads share the same process address space, each thread has its own stack. The stack is used to store local variables, return addresses, and other function call information that is specific to a thread.

Option D: Threads belonging to a process are by default not protected from each other

This statement is true. Threads within the same process share the same address space and can access the same data. This means that one thread can potentially read, write, or corrupt the data used by another thread unless proper synchronization mechanisms (like mutexes or semaphores) are used.

So, the correct answer is:

Option D

content: Consider three concurrent processes P1, P2 and P3 as shown below, which access a shared variable D that has been initialized to 100. The processes are executed on a uniprocessor system running a time-shared operating system. If the minimum and maximum possible values of D after the three processes have completed execution are X and Y respectively, then the value of Y–X is _________.
explanation:
Case 1 :

Let initially P2 reads D = 100 and then got preempted.

Then P1 executes D = D + 20, so now D = 120.

Then P3 executes D = D + 10, so now D = 130.

Initially when P2 got preempted the value of D was = 100.

Now P2 executes D = D - 50 = 100 - 50 = 50

P2 writes D = 50 final value. This is minimum.

Case 2 :

Let initially P1 reads D = 100 and then got preempted.

Yhen P3 reads D = 100 and then got preempted.

Then P2 reads D = 100 and executes D = D - 50 = 100 - 50 = 50 and writes it.

Then P1 executes D = D + 20 = 100 + 20 = 120

Then P3 reads D = 120 and executes D = D + 10 gives maximum value D = 130.

So, Y - X = 130 - 50 = 80.

content: A file is organized so that the ordering of data records is the same as or close to the ordering of data entries in some index. Then that index is called

options.content: Dense

content: Three concurrent processes X, Y, and Z execute three different code segments that access and update certain shared variables. Process X executes the P operation (i.e., wait) on semaphores a, b and c; process Y executes the P operation on semaphores b, c and d; process Z executes the P operation on semaphores c, d, and a before entering the respective code segments. After completing the execution of its code segment, each process invokes the V operation (i.e., signal) on its three semaphores. All semaphores are binary semaphores initialized to one. Which one of the following represents a deadlock-free order of invoking the P operations by the processes?

options.content:
X:P(a)P(b)P(c)
Y:P(b)P(c)P(d)
Z:P(c)P(d)P(a)

X:P(b)P(a)P(c)
Y:P(b)P(c)P(d)
Z:P(a)P(c)P(d)

X:P(b)P(a)P(c)
Y:P(c)P(b)P(d)
Z:P(a)P(c)P(d)

X:P(a)P(b)P(c)
Y:P(c)P(b)P(d)
Z:P(c)P(d)P(a)

content: A counting semaphore was initialized to 10. Then 6 P (wait) operations and 4 V (signal) operations were completed on this semaphore. The resulting value of the semaphore

content:
Consider three processes P1, P2, and P3 running identical code, as shown in the pseudocode below. A and B are two binary semaphores initialized to 1 and 0 , respectively. $X$ is a shared variable initialized to 0 . Each line in the pseudocode is executed atomically. Pseudocode of P1, P2, and P3

Wait(A);

Print(*);

X = X+1;

If (X == 2)

    {

        Print($);

        Signal(B);

    }

Signal(A);

Wait(B);

Print(#);

Signal(B);

Assume that any of the three processes can start to execute first and context switching can happen between these processes at any arbitrary time and in any arbitrary order. Which of the following patterns is/are possible to be generated as an outcome of the execution of these three processes?
explanation:
Option (d) is not possible: It is not possible to print *** one after another without printing $ after the second *. This is because, in the code, when the shared variable X becomes 2, the process must print $ and then signal the semaphore B. So, after printing the second *, $ must be printed before the third *.

All other options are possible: The other output patterns can happen because, depending on the order in which the processes run, it is possible for $ to be printed after two * are printed, and then for # and other outputs to follow as per the semaphore signals.

content:
Consider the following two threads T1 and T2 that update two shared variables a and b. Assume that initially $a = 1$ and $b = 1$. Though context switching between threads can happen at any time, each statement of T1 or T2 is executed atomically without interruption.

T1:
$a = a + 1$;
$b = b + 1$;

T2:
$b = 2 * b$;
$a = 2 * a$;

Which one of the following options lists all the possible combinations of values of a and b after both T1 and T2 finish execution?
explanation:
Let's analyze the execution of threads T1 and T2, given the initial values of the shared variables $$a = 1$$ and $$b = 1$$. Suppose context switching can happen at any time, but each statement of T1 or T2 executes atomically.

Thread T1 executes:
1. $$a = a + 1$$;
2. $$b = b + 1$$;

Thread T2 executes:
1. $$b = 2 * b$$;
2. $$a = 2 * a$$;

Consider the sequences of execution:

Case 1: T1 executes first, followed by T2

1. $$a = a + 1 \Rightarrow a = 1 + 1 = 2$$
2. $$b = b + 1 \Rightarrow b = 1 + 1 = 2$$
3. $$b = 2 * b \Rightarrow b = 2 * 2 = 4$$
4. $$a = 2 * a \Rightarrow a = 2 * 2 = 4$$
Final values: $$a = 4$$, $$b = 4$$

Case 2: T2 executes first, followed by T1

1. $$b = 2 * b \Rightarrow b = 2 * 1 = 2$$
2. $$a = 2 * a \Rightarrow a = 2 * 1 = 2$$
3. $$a = a + 1 \Rightarrow a = 2 + 1 = 3$$
4. $$b = b + 1 \Rightarrow b = 2 + 1 = 3$$
Final values: $$a = 3$$, $$b = 3$$

Case 3: Mixed execution

Scenario 1: T1's first statement, T2 executes, T1's second statement
1. $$a = a + 1 \Rightarrow a = 1 + 1 = 2$$
2. $$b = 2 * b \Rightarrow b = 2 * 1 = 2$$
3. $$a = 2 * a \Rightarrow a = 2 * 2 = 4$$
4. $$b = b + 1 \Rightarrow b = 2 + 1 = 3$$
Final values: $$a = 4$$, $$b = 3$$

Scenario 2: T2's first statement, T1 executes, T2's second statement
1. $$b = 2 * b \Rightarrow b = 2 * 1 = 2$$
2. $$a = a + 1 \Rightarrow a = 1 + 1 = 2$$
3. $$b = b + 1 \Rightarrow b = 2 + 1 = 3$$
4. $$a = 2 * a \Rightarrow a = 2 * 2 = 4$$
Final values: $$a = 4$$, $$b = 3$$

A thorough analysis shows that no other significant ordering yields different final values.

Combining all possible final values from these sequences, the possible combinations of $$a$$ and $$b$$ after both T1 and T2 finish execution are:

$$(a = 4, b = 4), (a = 3, b = 3), (a = 4, b = 3)$$

Thus, the correct option is:

Option A: $(a = 4, b = 4); (a = 3, b = 3); (a = 4, b = 3)$

content:
Consider the following code snippet using the fork() and wait() system calls. Assume that the code compiles and runs correctly, and that the system calls run successfully without any errors.

int x = 3;
while(x > 0) {
  fork();
  printf("hello");
  wait(NULL);
  x--;
}

The total number of times the printf statement is executed is _______
explanation:
To determine the total number of times the printf statement is executed, we need to analyze the behavior of the fork() system call and the structure of the loop.

The fork() system call creates a new process, which is a copy of the current process. Each call to fork() will result in the creation of a child process. Importantly, both the parent and the child process will continue executing the code following the fork() call.

Let's break down the execution step-by-step:

Initially:

int x = 3;

The loop condition while(x > 0) will execute three times because the initial value of x is 3 and it decrements by 1 each iteration.

Here's how the processes are created and the printf calls are executed:

  1. 1st Iteration (x = 3):
    • Parent process (P0) starts with x = 3.
    • P0 calls fork() creating child process (P1).
    • Both P0 and P1 will execute printf("hello").
    • Then both will call wait(NULL) (Only parent will wait).
    • P0 and P1 decrement their own x (both become x = 2).
  2. 2nd Iteration (x = 2):
    • P0 and P1 now have x = 2.
    • P0 calls fork() creating a new child (P2).
    • P1 calls fork() creating a new child (P3).
    • We now have P0, P1, P2, and P3.
    • All four processes execute printf("hello").
    • P0 and P1 will call wait(NULL) (Only parents will wait).
    • Each process decrements its own x (all become x = 1).
  3. 3rd Iteration (x = 1):
    • P0, P1, P2, and P3 now have x = 1.
    • P0 calls fork() creating a new child (P4).
    • P1 calls fork() creating a new child (P5).
    • P2 calls fork() creating a new child (P6).
    • P3 calls fork() creating a new child (P7).
    • We now have P0, P1, P2, P3, P4, P5, P6, and P7.
    • All eight processes execute printf("hello").
    • P0, P1, P2, and P3 will call wait(NULL) (Only parents will wait).
    • Each process decrements its own x (all become x = 0).

To summarize, we have the following counts of "hello" printed:

  • 1st Iteration: 2 "hello"
  • 2nd Iteration: 4 "hello"
  • 3rd Iteration: 8 "hello"

The total number of times the printf statement is executed is:

$$\begin{aligned} 2 + 4 + 8 = 14 \end{aligned}$$

Therefore, the total number of times the printf statement is executed is 14.

content:
Consider the two functions incr and decr shown below.


incr() {
  wait(s);
  X = X+1;
  signal(s);
}

decr() {
   wait(s);
   X = X-1;
   signal(s);
}

There are 5 threads each invoking incr once, and 3 threads each invoking decr once, on the same shared variable X. The initial value of X is 10.

Suppose there are two implementations of the semaphore s, as follows:

I-1: s is a binary semaphore initialized to 1.

I-2: s is a counting semaphore initialized to 2.

Let V1, V2 be the values of X at the end of execution of all the threads with implementations I-1, I-2, respectively.

Which one of the following choices corresponds to the minimum possible values of V1, V2, respectively?
explanation:
To determine the minimum possible values of $ V_1 $ and $ V_2 $ under the given implementations of the semaphore, let's analyze the behavior of the functions:

With Implementation I-1 (Binary Semaphore):

The binary semaphore ensures mutual exclusion, meaning that only one thread can access the critical section (the block of code between wait(s) and signal(s)) at a time.

Since there are 5 incr() operations and 3 decr() operations on the shared variable $ X $, and these operations execute one-by-one due to the mutual exclusive nature guaranteed by the binary semaphore, the operations will be serialized correctly.

Initial value of $ X $ is 10:

$ X_{\text{final}} = 10 + 5\ (\text{incr}) - 3\ (\text{decr}) = 10 + 2 = 12 $

Thus, $ V_1 = 12 $.

With Implementation I-2 (Counting Semaphore):

A counting semaphore initialized to 2 allows up to 2 threads to execute their critical sections concurrently. This means:

Two increments (incr()) or decrements (decr()) or one increment and one decrement can occur at the same time.

If threads are not synchronized, interleaving can potentially cause race conditions and hence unpredictable results.

Considering the worst-case interleaving:

Let's assume that initially the two decr() operations run concurrently, potentially leading to decreased values of $ X $ before all incr() operations get a chance to increase it, causing:

Two decr():

$ X = 10 - 1 - 1 = 8 $

Following that, all incr() operations and remaining decr() can cause:

Three incr() and one decr() on top of the previous result:

$ X = 8 + 1 + 1 + 1 - 1 = 10 $

However, due to potential adverse scheduling and race conditions, the minimum result can further be decreased based on specific thread scheduling:

$ X_{\text{worst-case}} = 10 + 0\ (\text{net result balance swung by interleavings}) - 3\ (\text{decr}) = 7 $

Thus, $ V_2 $ can be as low as 7 due to race conditions not protected by the counting semaphore.

Therefore, the correct choice for the minimum possible values of $ V_1 $ and $ V_2 $ is:

Option C: 12, 7

content:
Consider a computer system with multiple shared resource types, with one instance per resource type. Each instance can be owned by only one process at a time. Owning and freeing of resources are done by holding a global lock (L). The following scheme is used to own a resource instance:

function OWNRESOURCES(Resource R)

Acquire lock L / / a global lock

if R is available then

Acquire R

Release lock L

else

if R is owned by another process P then

Terminate P, after releasing all resources owned by P

Acquire R

Restart P

Release lock L

end if

end if

end function

Which of the following choice(s) about the above scheme is/are correct?

options.content: The scheme may lead to starvation.
The scheme may lead to live-lock.
The scheme ensures that deadlocks will not occur.
The scheme violates the mutual exclusion property.

explanation:
function OWNRESOURCES(Resource R)

Acquire lock L / / a global lock

if R is available then

Acquire R

Release lock L

else

if R is owned by another process P then

Terminate P, after releasing all resources owned by P

Acquire R

Restart P

Release lock L

end if

end if

end function

Mutual exclusion is not violated.

Also, there will be no deadlock because of forceful pre-emption of resources.

This may lead to starvation if the process is keeps on coming and pre-empting each other like P1 is pre-empted by P2 and P2 is preempted by P3.

Live-lock is also possible due to continuous pre-emption of resources.

For option (c) consider two processes P1 and P2 now P1 enter the code acquires lock and resource.

Now P2 enters the else part kills P1 and acquire R and restart P1 Now P1 again acquire lock and kills the process P2 this continues creating a live lock scenario but there is ambiguity in the code since "Release R" is not written anywhere so ambiguity is regarding how the process will release Resource R. According to the code, the only way to release the resource is by getting killed.

Hence, the correct options are (A), (B) and (C).

content:
Consider the following multi-threaded code segment (in a mix of C and pseudocode), invoked by two processes P1 and P2, and each of the processes spawns two threads T1 and T2:

int x = 0; // global

Lock L1; // global

main() {

create a thread to execute foo(); // Thread T1

create a thread to execute foo(); // Thread T2

wait for the two threads to finish execution;

print (x);}

foo() {

int y = 0;

Acquire L1;

x = x + 1;

y = y + 1;

Release L1;

print (y); }

Which of the following statement(s) is/are correct ?

options.content: Both T1 and T2, in both the processes, will print the value of y as 1.
At least one of P1 and P2 will print the value of x as 4
Both P1 and P2 will print the value of x as 2.
At least one of the threads will print the value of y as 2.

explanation:
To determine which statements are correct, we'll analyze the code and understand how variables x and y change during execution.

Understanding the Code:

Global Variables (per process):

int x = 0;

x is a global variable within each process.

Lock L1;

L1 is a lock within each process.

Process Structure:

Each process (P1 and P2) does the following:

Creates two threads (T1 and T2).

Each thread executes the foo() function.

After both threads finish, the process prints the value of x.

Function foo():

  foo() {

      int y = 0;

      Acquire L1;

      x = x + 1;

      y = y + 1;

      Release L1;

      print(y);

  }

Analyzing Each Statement:

Option A:

Statement: Both T1 and T2, in both the processes, will print the value of y as 1.

Analysis:

In foo(), y is a local variable initialized to 0.

Inside the critical section:

x = x + 1; (increments the global x)

y = y + 1; (increments the local y)

After releasing the lock, the thread prints y.

Since y starts at 0 and is incremented once:

Each thread will print y = 1.

Conclusion: Option A is correct.

Option B:

Statement: At least one of P1 and P2 will print the value of x as 4.

Analysis:

Since x is global within each process, P1 and P2 have their own separate x.

In each process:

Both T1 and T2 increment x by 1.

So, x becomes 2 in each process.

There is no sharing of x between P1 and P2.

Therefore, neither P1 nor P2 will print x as 4.

Conclusion: Option B is incorrect.

Option C:

Statement: Both P1 and P2 will print the value of x as 2.

Analysis:

As established, in each process:

x starts at 0.

Both threads increment x once.

After both threads finish, x = 2.

Each process prints x = 2.

Conclusion: Option C is correct.

Option D:

Statement: At least one of the threads will print the value of y as 2.

Analysis:

y is a local variable in foo(), initialized to 0 each time foo() is called.

It is incremented once in each call to foo().

There is no way for y to become 2 in any thread.

Conclusion: Option D is incorrect.

Final Answer:

Options A and C are correct.

Options B and D are incorrect.

Answer: Options A and C are correct.

content:
Each of a set of n processes executes the following code using two semaphores a and b initialized to 1 and 0, respectively. Assume that count is a shared variable
What does the code achieve?

options.content: It ensures that all processes execute CODE SECTION P mutually exclusively.
It ensures that at most two processes are in CODE SECTION Q at any time.
It ensures that no process executes CODE SECTION Q before every process has finished CODE SECTION P.
It ensures that at most n-1 processes are in CODE SECTION P at any time.

explanation:
Given :

All the processes running the given code will remain block due to wait (b) until the value the value of count becomes n.

When the value of count will become equals ton, value of b changes to 1 which will subsequently allow a process to go into process section θ.

Hence, no process can go to section θ until all the process executes code sati on P.

Hence, the correct option is (C).

options.content:
Exit X (R, S){
  P(R);
  V(S);
}
Entry Y (R, S){
  P(S);
  V(R);
}

Exit X (R, S){
  V(R);
  V(S);
}
Entry Y (R, S){
  P(R);
  P(S);
}

Exit X (R, S){
  P(S);
  V(R);
}
Entry Y (R, S){
  V(S);
  P(R);
}

Exit X (R, S){
  V(R);
  P(S);
}
Entry Y (R, S){
  V(S);
  P(R);
}

content: A shared variable x, initialized to zero, is operated on by four concurrent processes W, X, Y, Z as follows. Each of the processes W and X reads x from memory, increments by one, stores it to memory, and then terminates. Each of the processes Y and Z reads x from memory, decrements by two, stores it to memory, and then terminates. Each process before reading x invokes the P operation (i.e., wait) on a counting semaphore S and invokes the V operation (i.e., signal) on the semaphore S after storing x to memory. Semaphore S is initialized to two. What is the maximum possible value of x after all processes complete execution?

options.content: fails as L can overflow
fails as L can take on a non-zero value when the lock is actually available
works correctly but may starve some processes
works correctly without starvation

content: A solution to the Dining Philosophers Problem which avoids deadlock is

ensure that all philosophers pick up the right fork before the left fork
ensure that one particular philosopher picks up the left fork before the right fork, and that all other philosophers pick up the right fork before the left fork

content:
The concurrent programming constructs fork and join are as below: Fork (label) which creates a new process executing from the specified label join (variable) which decrements the specified synchronization variable (by 1) and terminates the process if the new value is not 0. Show the precedence graph for S1, S2, S3, S4 and S5 of the concurrent program below.
N = 2
M = 2
fork L3
fork L4
S1
L1 : join N
S3
L2: join M
S5
L3:S2
goto L1
L4:S4
goto L2
next:
At a particular time of computation the value of a counting semaphore is 7. Then 20 P operations and 15 V operations were completed on this semaphore. The resulting value of the semaphore is:
Semaphore operations are atomic because they are implemented within the OS
A critical region is:

options.content: One which is enclosed by a pair of P and V operations on semaphores
A program segment that has not been proved bug-free
A program segment that often causes unexpected system crashes
A program segment where shared resources are accessed
